excerpt: Output script descriptors are a simple language used to describe collections of output scripts. They support all standard script types — pkh, wpkh, sh, wsh, tr — and key origin information including master fingerprint and derivation path. Descriptors are designed to be the canonical interchange format for wallet configurations.
body:
Source excerpt:
Output script descriptors are a simple language used to describe collections of output scripts. They support all standard script types — pkh, wpkh, sh, wsh, tr — and key origin information including master fingerprint and derivation path. Descriptors are designed to be the canonical interchange format for wallet configurations.

---

Cited by leaf "Output descriptors":
An output descriptor is a single string that fully describes how to derive and recognize the addresses of a wallet — the script type, the keys involved, and where they came from. For example: `wpkh([d34db33f/84'/0'/0']xpub6Cab.../0/*)#3vhfv5h5` is a watch-only native-segwit single-sig wallet, derived at the standard BIP84 account-zero path, with the master fingerprint and a checksum so a typo doesn't load the wrong wallet [1]. Before descriptors, every wallet had its own private export format and importing a watch-only setup from one wallet into another was a chronic source of misery and silent loss.

The motivation is unambiguous communication. A multisig setup, a taproot script tree, or a complex contract has many ways to be encoded and only one of them is the one you actually have funds on. Descriptors make the spec self-contained: anyone with the descriptor string can generate the same address sequence, watch the same coins, and (if they have the relevant private keys) sign the same transactions [2]. Bitcoin Core has used descriptors as its native wallet format since v0.21; modern signers (the open-source ones especially) export and import descriptors directly.

The Miniscript and Tapscript descriptor extensions go further — they let you describe arbitrary spending policies in a language that compiles to optimal Bitcoin Script and that a wallet can analyze ("can I spend this? do I need a co-signer? does the timelock allow this now?") without having to implement custom logic per contract type [3]. Descriptors are the unsexy plumbing that quietly took multisig, taproot, and complex custody from "works for one specific wallet's blob format" to "works as long as your wallet implements the spec."
